caveats: a: The occupation source is the three-digit NAICS 459000 parent and includes several materially different retail industries.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares, and self-employed owners are excluded.
a: Current automation in POS, marketplaces, online cataloging, and loyalty systems is not separately observed.
rho: BLS projections span all industries and ten years rather than this lens and five years.
rho: GameStop is far larger than the target population, and its cost reduction included store closures and divestitures rather than AI alone.
rho: Some workforce change reflects self-checkout and e-commerce, not AI substitution.
e: The parent occupation mix includes unrelated retail segments and cannot identify firm-level recurring revenue.
e: GameStop demonstrates model existence but is not representative of independent LMM firms.
e: The frozen n input is missing, so this share cannot be converted to an eligible firm count in the packet.
s5: The Census age statistic is cross-industry, owner-based, and reflects data year 2018 respondents.
s5: BizBuySell is a marketplace sample and skews toward Main Street transactions below the target EBITDA band.
s5: Older ownership does not imply an external qualifying sale within five years.
q: GameStop and toy manufacturers are larger and more channel-diverse than the target firms.
q: Merchandise margin, licensing economics, and service-fee retention differ substantially.
q: The estimate cannot fully separate AI-derived savings from inventory mix and store-footprint actions.
d5: One-year toy unit growth followed two weaker years and may not persist.
d5: Category growth is highly concentrated in licensed and collectible properties with short or cyclical life spans.
d5: GameStop's mix is not representative of traditional toys, crafts, models, or independent tabletop stores.
o: A distressed public chain's footprint changes may not generalize to profitable specialist operators.
o: Digital substitution differs sharply between video games, tabletop play, models, crafts, toys, and collectibles.
o: The estimate applies only to the narrowed service-bearing basket, not ordinary product sales.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumer, collector, club, school, and institutional relationships combining merchandise margin with memberships, grading or authentication fees, repair or rental charges, instruction, events, or repeat program supply; much product revenue remains transactional and seasonal.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is predominantly transactional retail but includes a small, distinct subset built around recurring memberships, collector services, repair, rental, instruction, organized play, or institutional programs. The lens is narrowed to that subset for business-model coherence, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure merchandise stores and e-commerce sellers, product manufacturers and publishers, arcades and entertainment venues classified outside retail, captive operations, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without a recurring or repeat outsourced-service relationship.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market hobby, toy, and game retailers with repeat external-customer services such as paid memberships, grading or authentication intermediation, repair, rental, instruction, institutional programs, or organized-play operations supported by recurring fees or repeat service purchases.

a.bridge: BLS publishes occupation shares for the broader NAICS 459000 parent, not 459120, and does not measure task-level AI exposure. The estimate maps high exposure to customer communications, product research, listing content, order administration, bookkeeping, marketing, scheduling, and routine checkout; partial exposure to sales and instruction; and low exposure to physical events, repair, stocking, and exception handling. Parent-industry floral, sporting-goods, book, and miscellaneous retailers may distort the mix in either direction.
caveats: The occupation source is the three-digit NAICS 459000 parent and includes several materially different retail industries.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares, and self-employed owners are excluded.
Current automation in POS, marketplaces, online cataloging, and loyalty systems is not separately observed.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-linked time studies at independent game, hobby, collectible, and toy retailers, separated by membership, grading, event, repair, and institutional-service workflows, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The broader retail group is dominated by sales occupations at 56.80%, including retail salespersons at 37.94% and cashiers at 9.17%; office and administrative support is 9.99%. AI can assist recommendation, cataloging, collectibles research, customer service, event promotion, order processing, and back-office workflows, but the qualifying services also depend on physical handling, community facilitation, trust, and specialist judgment.

d5.bridge: Circana measures toy units and dollars for the U.S. retail market, while GameStop reports a video-game and collectibles mix; neither measures constant-price quantity for the narrowed recurring-service basket. The estimate uses 2025 toy unit growth of 3% as a recent positive signal but tempers it because the prior two years stagnated or declined and 92% of dollar growth came from three categories. Service demand could track enthusiast participation and collecting more closely than total toy units but remains discretionary and trend-sensitive.
caveats: One-year toy unit growth followed two weaker years and may not persist.
Category growth is highly concentrated in licensed and collectible properties with short or cyclical life spans.
GameStop's mix is not representative of traditional toys, crafts, models, or independent tabletop stores.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-price transaction volumes for memberships, organized play, grading submissions, repairs, rentals, and institutional programs from merchant POS systems and service vendors would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: U.S. toy unit sales rose 3% in 2025 and total dollars rose 6%, but growth was concentrated in games/puzzles, building sets, and explorative toys, especially Pokémon and trading cards. GameStop's 2025 collectibles sales rose 47.7% while software fell 27.5%, illustrating category rotation rather than uniform growth. A near-flat base with a wide range is therefore appropriate.

e.bridge: The parent-industry OEWS mix and GameStop disclosures identify service-adjacent roles and models, not eligible-firm share. The estimate uses low parent shares for self-enrichment teachers (0.62%), service sales representatives (0.37%), counter/rental clerks (0.28%), and repair occupations (2.76%), plus direct evidence that a major retailer operates paid loyalty and grading services, to bound a small service-bearing subset. Parent-industry mixing likely overstates repair from sporting goods and understates organized-play work performed by ordinary sales staff.
caveats: The parent occupation mix includes unrelated retail segments and cannot identify firm-level recurring revenue.
GameStop demonstrates model existence but is not representative of independent LMM firms.
The frozen n input is missing, so this share cannot be converted to an eligible firm count in the packet.
e.change_evidence: A merchant-level census using websites, POS category data, association rosters, and interviews to measure recurring service revenue and EBITDA would directly estimate eligibility.
e.rationale: Most hobby, toy, and game retailers sell discretionary products transactionally. A narrow subset earns repeat fees or supports persistent relationships through memberships, grading/authentication, repair, rental, instruction, events, or institutional programs; even there, merchandise often remains the economic center.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[45]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

o.bridge: GameStop's channel disclosures and BLS occupation projections show substitution pathways but do not measure the year-five operator-required share for qualifying LMM firms. The estimate treats digital downloads, publisher stores, online marketplaces, self-service checkout, automated recommendations, and digital grading platforms as displacement routes, while retaining operator need for custody, provenance, physical repair, instruction, live community, institutional accountability, and exception resolution.
caveats: A distressed public chain's footprint changes may not generalize to profitable specialist operators.
Digital substitution differs sharply between video games, tabletop play, models, crafts, toys, and collectibles.
The estimate applies only to the narrowed service-bearing basket, not ordinary product sales.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal customer migration and channel-share data for grading, repair, rental, instruction, organized play, and membership services—including digital-only substitutes—would materially refine the estimate.
o.rationale: Digital delivery can fully eliminate some physical game transactions, and online platforms can automate discovery, membership, and portions of collectibles trading. Yet the narrowed lens emphasizes physical or trust-based services. GameStop closed 727 U.S. stores in fiscal 2025 while calling the remaining footprint core logistics infrastructure and launching a digital graded-card platform, evidence that operator roles persist but can migrate and consolidate.

q.bridge: Public-company filings describe competitive pricing, supplier power, digital channels, and category economics rather than retained AI benefit at LMM retailers. The estimate applies those forces to posted merchandise prices and discretionary service fees, allowing more retention for trusted local grading, repair, or community services and less for easily compared products, memberships, and online-enabled services.
caveats: GameStop and toy manufacturers are larger and more channel-diverse than the target firms.
Merchandise margin, licensing economics, and service-fee retention differ substantially.
The estimate cannot fully separate AI-derived savings from inventory mix and store-footprint actions.
q.change_evidence: Service-level pricing histories, membership renewals, bid data, and gross-margin bridges before and after automation at independent hobby and game retailers would identify pass-through and competitive sharing.
q.rationale: Savings are not automatically passed through under posted retail and fee pricing, so the operator can initially retain them. Over renewals and competitive responses, marketplaces, mass merchants, publisher/manufacturer channels, digital substitutes, and price-sensitive hobbyists should capture part of the benefit. GameStop explicitly warns that price reductions or increased spending may be required to maintain customers.

rho.bridge: BLS national occupation projections and GameStop's large-company cost actions indicate automation and workflow change but do not directly measure five-year implementation among qualifying LMM firms. The estimate translates cashier and customer-service declines plus observed retail cost optimization into an implementation share, then discounts for fragmented systems, limited capital, inventory-data quality, community-service expectations, and human review needs.
caveats: BLS projections span all industries and ten years rather than this lens and five years.
GameStop is far larger than the target population, and its cost reduction included store closures and divestitures rather than AI alone.
Some workforce change reflects self-checkout and e-commerce, not AI substitution.
rho.change_evidence: Cohort adoption and audited labor-hour data from hobby-retail POS, marketplace, grading, loyalty, and event-management vendors over five years would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: BLS projects cashier employment down 10% and customer-service representatives down 5% from 2024 to 2034, while GameStop reduced labor-related, consulting, and marketing costs as part of cost optimization. Independent hobby and game retailers can adopt off-the-shelf AI for content, service, and administration, but full workflow redesign is constrained by thin management capacity and trust-sensitive collector and community interactions.

s5.bridge: Census owner-age evidence and BizBuySell retail transactions cover broad small employer and retail populations, not qualifying hobby/toy/game firms in the target EBITDA band. The estimate converts broad succession pressure and observed retail liquidity into a five-year control-transfer probability, reducing for closures, internal transfers, owner-dependent community businesses, inventory risk, and transactions below the EBITDA band.
caveats: The Census age statistic is cross-industry, owner-based, and reflects data year 2018 respondents.
BizBuySell is a marketplace sample and skews toward Main Street transactions below the target EBITDA band.
Older ownership does not imply an external qualifying sale within five years.
s5.change_evidence: Closed transaction and sell-side mandate data from DealStats, Axial, GF Data, game/hobby specialist brokers, and SBA lenders, matched to EBITDA, owner age, and service mix, would materially refine the probability.
s5.rationale: Census reported 51% of responding employer-business owners age 55 or older, while BizBuySell records an active independent-retail resale market. However, the benchmark median owner earnings of $131,498 is far below the target band, and hobby businesses may depend heavily on owner expertise, customer trust, inventory provenance, and community relationships.

principal_driver: Enthusiast trust, physical custody, specialist service, and recurring community relationships can preserve a local operator while administrative workflows become AI-assisted.
principal_weakness: Recurring outsourced-service firms are a tiny, poorly measured subset of a product-led and highly trend-sensitive retail industry.

sources.supports: Parent-industry occupation shares: sales 56.80%, retail salespersons 37.94%, cashiers 9.17%, office and administrative support 9.99%, self-enrichment teachers 0.62%, service sales representatives 0.37%, counter/rental clerks 0.28%, and installation, maintenance, and repair 2.76%.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 459000

sources.supports: Cashier employment is projected to decline 10% from 2024 to 2034, with BLS citing self-service checkout and increasing online sales.
sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Cashiers

sources.supports: Customer-service-representative employment is projected to decline 5% from 2024 to 2034; duties include handling complaints, orders, questions, payments, returns, and account changes.
sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Customer Service Representatives

sources.supports: GameStop disclosed intense multichannel competition, continued digital-game substitution, 727 U.S. store closures, a digital graded-card platform, 47.7% collectibles growth, 27.5% software decline, and reduced labor-related, consulting, and marketing costs.
sources.title: GameStop Corp. Form 10-K for fiscal year ended January 31, 2026

sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey, data year 2018, found 51% of responding U.S. employer-business owners were age 55 or older, with stated representativeness limits.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Independent retail transactions reported to BizBuySell had a 2025 median sale price of $273,647; the benchmark reports median revenue of $720,000, median owner earnings of $131,498, and 165 median days on market.
sources.title: Retail Business Valuation Multiples and Financial Benchmarks

sources.supports: U.S. toy dollar sales grew 6% and units 3% in 2025 after two years of stagnation and decline; three supercategories drove 92% of growth, and games and puzzles rose 37%.
sources.title: U.S. Toy Industry Returns to Growth in 2025, Circana Reports

title: Hobby, Toy, and Game Retailers
